content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Timothy Hodson 
Consultant 
Senator Milton Marks 
October 2, 1989 
Senate Committee on Elections 
State Capitol, Room 5051 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Hodson: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-336 
In August 1988, you first wrote to us concerning the effect 
of Government Code section 85306, as added by Proposition 73,1/ 
on the use of campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989. 
This topic has been the subject of litigation for the last several 
months. 
We have included your name on our mailing list for 
Propositions 68 and 73, and thereby attempted to keep you informed 
of the Commission's interpretation of the law during this time 
period. 
The purpose o~ this letter is to inform you of the most 
recent development on this issue. 
On September 14, 1989, a federal court ruled that section 
85306 is unconstitutional and enjoined the Fair Political 
Practices Commission from enforcing that law. 
(Service Employees 
International Union v. Fair Political Practices Commission, Case 
No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of 
California.) 
A copy of the court order is enclosed. 
Accordingly, campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 
may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective 
office. 
We caution that candidates and elected officers who 
possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any 
other candidate for elective office. 
(Section 85304.) 
We also 
caution that these funds may not be used for personal 
expenditures, as defined in Elections Code sections 12400-12407. 
Attached is a Commission advice letter which explains the 
procedures for transferring these funds to a current campaign 
account. 
proposition 73 was adopted by the voters in June 1988. 
It 
amended the Political Reform Act (Government Code sections 81000-
91015.) 
All references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-88-336 
Page 2 
If you have any questions concerning this letter, please 
contact the Commission's Technical Assistance and Analysis 
Division at (916) 322-5662. 
Sincerely, 
~_ . {)h'-f)VI1v---
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Timothy Hodson 
Chief Consultant 
Senate Committee onElections 
state capitol, Room 5051 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Hodson: 
September 2, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 88-336 
Your letter requesting advice concerning the recently 
enacted campaign finance reform initiatives (Government Code 
section 85100, et ~) was received on August 30, 1988 by the 
Fair Political Practices commission. 
If you have any questions 
about your advice request, you may contact Kathryn Donovan, an 
attorney in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901 . 
Because of the delayed operative date of most of the 
provisions of these initiatives, and the numerous inquiries we 
are receiving relative to implementation, we are attempting to 
respond to questions regarding interpretation in an organized 
and efficient fashion. 
Due to the volume of requests and 
complexity of the issues involved, we will answer your question 
in a timely manner, but not within the usual twenty-one working 
days. 
(Government Code Section 83114(b).) 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
You also should be aware 
that your letter and our response are public records which may 
be disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for 
disclosure. 
GWB:plh:3

sections.question: 1. Does Proposition 73 prohibit or restrict the payment, after January 1, 1989, of expenses incurred prior to January 1, 1989 (i.e., accrued expenses for goods and/or services received prior to January 1, 1989, not pre-payments)? 2. May campaign funds held by a person on the effective date of Chapter 5 [Proposition 73] be used to pay debts incurred prior to January 1, 1989? 3. May campaign funds received prior to the operative date of Proposition 73 be used to pay debts after January 1, 1989? If so, would a ballot measure committee have to be formed and subject to the provisions set forth by the Commission? 6. [Regarding ballot measure funds] If not, would the use of such funds be subject to the provisions of Proposition 73 or would the limitations be determined by United States Supreme Court regarding initiatives campaigns (e.g. First National Bank v. Bellotti)?
sections.conclusion: On September 14, 1989, a federal court ruled that section 85306 is unconstitutional and enjoined the Fair Political Practices Commission from enforcing that law. Accordingly, campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective office. However, candidates and elected officers who possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any other candidate for elective office (Section 85304), and these funds may not be used for personal expenditures as defined in Elections Code sections 12400-12407.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asked whether Proposition 73 restricts the use of campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989, including whether such funds could be used to pay pre-January 1989 debts, and whether campaign funds related to ballot measures would be subject to new restrictions or Constitutional limitations on contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A federal court ruled section 85306 unconstitutional, allowing campaign funds received before January 1, 1989 to be used to support or oppose candidacies, but prohibiting their use for contributions to other candidates or personal expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes three separate sections: (1) the original incoming request from August 30, 1988; (2) FPPC acknowledgment letter from September 2, 1988; and (3) FPPC final response from October 2, 1989. The final response includes references to enclosed court order and another commission advice letter. Some questions in the original request appear truncated in the source document but substantive content is discernible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does Proposition 73 prohibit or restrict the payment, after January 1, 1989, of expenses incurred prior to January 1, 1989 (i.e., accrued expenses for goods and/or services received prior to January 1, 1989, not pre-payments)? 2. May campaign funds held by a person on the effective date of Chapter 5 [Proposition 73] be used to pay debts incurred prior to January 1, 1989? 3. May campaign funds received prior to the operative date of Proposition 73 be used to pay debts after January 1, 1989? If so, would a ballot measure committee have to be formed and subject to the provisions set forth by the Commission? 6. [Regarding ballot measure funds] If not, would the use of such funds be subject to the provisions of Proposition 73 or would the limitations be determined by United States Supreme Court regarding initiatives campaigns (e.g. First National Bank v. Bellotti)?

CONCLUSION: On September 14, 1989, a federal court ruled that section 85306 is unconstitutional and enjoined the Fair Political Practices Commission from enforcing that law. Accordingly, campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective office. However, candidates and elected officers who possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any other candidate for elective office (Section 85304), and these funds may not be used for personal expenditures as defined in Elections Code sections 12400-12407.

embedding.summary: This is the FPPC's response to advice requests regarding Proposition 73's application to pre-1989 campaign funds. The Commission informed the requester that a federal court had invalidated section 85306, permitting use of pre-1989 campaign funds for candidacy support while maintaining restrictions on candidate-to-candidate contributions and personal use.
